title: The clock that is always five minutes fast
summary: Every clock in Mei's grandmother's house is five minutes fast, and Mei finally asks why.
body:
In my grandmother's house, every clock is wrong. They are all five minutes fast. The kitchen clock, the wall clock, even the small clock by her bed. All of them say the wrong time.

When I was a child, this made me angry. I was late for school once. I trusted the clock. But the clock was five minutes fast, so I left early. Then I waited at the bus stop for a long time. The bus came at the real time, not the clock time.

Last year, I asked her. 'Grandmother, why are all your clocks fast? It is confusing.'

She laughed. 'Your grandfather did it,' she said. 'When we were young, he was always late. Late for work. Late for dinner. Late for everything. So I made all the clocks fast. Then he was on time.'

'But Grandfather died ten years ago,' I said softly.

She was quiet. 'Yes. He did.'

'So why keep the clocks fast now?'

She looked at the kitchen clock. 'Because when I look at the clock and it is fast, I remember why. I remember him. I remember a young man who could not be on time. If I fix the clocks, the time is right. But he is more gone.'

I did not know what to say. Now I am older. I understand. Some wrong things are not mistakes. They are a way to keep someone near.

annotations.meaningVi: nhanh năm phút (chạy nhanh hơn giờ thật)
mất đi nhiều hơn (cách nói của bà)
giữ ai đó ở gần (trong lòng)

grammarNotes.title: Past simple for finished actions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì quá khứ đơn cho hành động đã xong trong quá khứ. Trong bài: 'I asked her.' / 'She laughed.' / 'Grandfather died ten years ago.' Động từ thường thêm -ed (asked, laughed), nhưng nhiều động từ bất quy tắc đổi hẳn: 'die → died', 'make → made', 'be → was'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the past simple for finished past actions. From the passage: 'I asked her.' / 'She laughed.' / 'Grandfather died ten years ago.' Regular verbs add -ed (asked, laughed), but many irregular verbs change form: 'make → made', 'be → was'.

translationVi:
Trong nhà bà tôi, mọi chiếc đồng hồ đều sai. Tất cả đều nhanh năm phút. Đồng hồ bếp, đồng hồ treo tường, cả chiếc đồng hồ nhỏ cạnh giường bà. Tất cả đều chỉ sai giờ.

Hồi tôi còn bé, chuyện này làm tôi bực mình. Có lần tôi đi học muộn. Tôi tin vào đồng hồ. Nhưng đồng hồ nhanh năm phút, nên tôi ra khỏi nhà sớm. Rồi tôi đứng chờ ở trạm xe buýt rất lâu. Xe đến đúng giờ thật, không phải giờ đồng hồ.

Năm ngoái, tôi hỏi bà. 'Bà ơi, sao đồng hồ nhà mình nhanh hết vậy? Dễ nhầm lắm.'

Bà cười. 'Ông cháu làm đấy,' bà nói. 'Hồi bọn ta còn trẻ, ông lúc nào cũng muộn. Muộn làm. Muộn ăn tối. Muộn mọi thứ. Nên ta chỉnh tất cả đồng hồ nhanh lên. Thế là ông đúng giờ.'

'Nhưng ông mất mười năm rồi mà,' tôi khẽ nói.

Bà lặng im. 'Ừ. Ông mất rồi.'

'Vậy giờ sao bà còn để đồng hồ nhanh?'

Bà nhìn chiếc đồng hồ bếp. 'Vì khi ta nhìn đồng hồ và nó nhanh, ta nhớ tại sao. Ta nhớ ông. Ta nhớ một chàng trai trẻ không bao giờ đúng giờ. Nếu ta chỉnh lại đồng hồ cho đúng, thì giờ đúng. Nhưng ông mất đi nhiều hơn.'

Tôi không biết nói gì. Giờ tôi lớn hơn rồi. Tôi hiểu. Có những cái sai không phải là lỗi lầm. Chúng là một cách để giữ ai đó ở gần.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with the clocks in the grandmother's house?
options: They are all five minutes fast

questions.prompt: What happened when the narrator was a child and trusted the clock?
options: They left early and waited a long time at the bus stop
They missed school for a week
questions.explanation: 'I left early. Then I waited at the bus stop for a long time.'

questions.prompt: Who first made the clocks fast?
questions.explanation: 'So I made all the clocks fast.' — the grandmother did it.

questions.prompt: Why did she first make the clocks fast?
options: Because the grandfather was always late
Because she liked the wrong time
questions.explanation: 'he was always late... So I made all the clocks fast. Then he was on time.'
questions.hint: Her story of when they were young.
The soft line in the middle.

questions.prompt: Why does she keep the clocks fast now?
options: The fast clocks make her remember him
questions.explanation: 'when I look at the clock and it is fast, I remember why. I remember him.'

questions.prompt: What does she fear will happen if she fixes the clocks?
options: The house will be too quiet
questions.explanation: 'If I fix the clocks, the time is right. But he is more gone.'

questions.prompt: What does 'some wrong things are not mistakes' mean here?
options: The fast clocks are kept on purpose, to hold a memory
questions.explanation: 'They are a way to keep someone near.'

questions.prompt: How did the narrator's feeling about the clocks change?
options: From childhood anger to grown-up understanding
They still find it only annoying
questions.explanation: 'this made me angry' as a child, but 'Now I am older. I understand.'
questions.hint: Compare the start and the end.

options: A small habit can carry love and memory for a lost person.
Clocks should always show the right time.
questions.explanation: The 'wrong' clocks keep the grandfather near.
questions.hint: What the wrong time really stands for.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The grandmother keeps a small 'wrong' thing to keep her husband near. Is there an object or habit in your home that looks pointless to others but holds a memory or a person for you?
